coredata.dc:description: Graphene is a promising electrode material that has been applied in capacitive charge storage. Compared to graphene powders, graphene films can be employed directly as electrodes without any additives. Severe restacking and aggregation of individual graphene nanosheets inevitably occur during the process of graphene due to van der Waals forces, especially for graphene films. Either pre-incorporation of an electrolyte involves complex crafts or introduction of hard separators compromises the performance of whole films. To overcome these drawbacks, we have pioneeringly developed molten salt reduction of graphene oxide films separated by carbon nanodots (CNDs). The incorporation of CNDs opens the channels between graphene oxide layers and leaves more defects during the molten salt treatment. Surprisingly, CNDs are sacrificed during subsequent pyrolysis, which do not compromise but enhance the performance of the films. Consequently, the sacrificed-CND graphene film in the KOH electrolyte much outperforms those graphene films obtained by other methods. The supercapattery based on the corresponding graphene powders exhibits a specific capacity of 139 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 at the near-commercial mass loading of 11.1 mg cm−2. The maximum specific energy of 369 Wh kg−1 can be achieved.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodot-sacrificed graphene films and powders treated by molten salt for high-performance capacitive charge storage

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene is a promising electrode material that has been applied in capacitive charge storage. Compared to graphene powders, graphene films can be employed directly as electrodes without any additives. Severe restacking and aggregation of individual graphene nanosheets inevitably occur during the process of graphene due to van der Waals forces, especially for graphene films. Either pre-incorporation of an electrolyte involves complex crafts or introduction of hard separators compromises the performance of whole films. To overcome these drawbacks, we have pioneeringly developed molten salt reduction of graphene oxide films separated by carbon nanodots (CNDs). The incorporation of CNDs opens the channels between graphene oxide layers and leaves more defects during the molten salt treatment. Surprisingly, CNDs are sacrificed during subsequent pyrolysis, which do not compromise but enhance the performance of the films. Consequently, the sacrificed-CND graphene film in the KOH electrolyte much outperforms those graphene films obtained by other methods. The supercapattery based on the corresponding graphene powders exhibits a specific capacity of 139 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 at the near-commercial mass loading of 11.1 mg cm−2. The maximum specific energy of 369 Wh kg−1 can be achieved.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene is a 2D monolayer of graphite that exhibits unique mechanical and physical properties, as well as a large theoretical specific surface area [1]. Chemically converted graphene, hereafter referred to as “graphene,” has been applied in capacitive charge storage, including supercapacitors and supercapatteries [2–5] which offer higher power density and longer lifetimes compared to rechargeable batteries [6–9]. With these distinctive advantages, they can be utilized in portable electronics and smart grids for renewable energy storage [10].

Conventionally, graphene powders can be prepared by reducing graphene oxide (GO) colloids with different reducing agents [11–13] or by pyrolysis of GO powders [14]. The stable graphene colloids can be transformed into free-standing flexible films by assembling individual reduced graphene oxide sheets through vacuum-assisted filtration [15,16]. Another method to produce graphene films is reduction of GO films obtained through vacuum-assisted filtration with reducing agents, such as HI and hydrazine hydrate [17,18]. Compared to graphene powders, graphene films exhibiting higher mechanical strength can serve directly as electrodes without the need of conductive additives or adhesives [19]. This feature reduces the parasitic weight of the cell components, thereby preventing potential reductions in coulombic efficiencies and specific and volumetric capacitive performance of the overall device.

However, due to van der Waals forces, severe restacking and aggregation of individual graphene nanosheets inevitably occur during the process of graphene, especially severely for bulk graphene films. Consequently, many of the unique advantages of individual sheets, such as high theoretical specific surface area and peculiar electron and ion transport properties, are not completely realized in graphene powders and further significantly decay in graphene films. These deteriorated properties render the actual capacitive performance of graphene films and powders far lower than the theoretical value of graphene. In particular, the reduction of GO films frequently fails to endow graphene films with qualified specific capacitive performance [17,20].

One of the most efficient methods to overcome the restacking of individual graphene nanosheets is utilizing hard substances as separators to be intercalated into graphene gallery. In order to open channels between graphene layers, conductive carbon black particles were used to prevent graphene from restacking in powders and films [21,22]. As a result, the specific capacitance of the graphene composite film was measured to be 138 F g−1 in alkaline aqueous electrolytes. Other hard separators, such as carbon nanotubes [23,24], MXenes [25–27], polymers [28–30] or even metal-organic frames [31], could be also introduced into graphene films. Unfortunately, incorporation of hard separators either decrease the specific capacitance or influence the other performances.

Employing water as a soft template appeared to address the problems specialized in bulk films. The residual water in wet hydrogel graphene films was exchanged with electrolyte solution before cell assembly. Pre-incorporation of the electrolyte could open channels between the graphene layers, resulting in superior performance of 215 F g−1 in the acidic electrolyte compared to lyophilized graphene films [32]. Further vacuum treatment of the electrolyte-pre-incorporated electrode could increase the volumetric capacitance of the graphene films [16]. However, these strategies are not sufficiently facile for scalable production. Firstly, the use of graphene colloids as precursors necessitates large-volume dilute GO colloids (0.25 mg mL−1) to ensure good dispersity of the resulting graphene colloids. Secondly, precise control of the amount of residual water in graphene films is difficult in the lengthy filtration. Thirdly, pre-incorporating an electrolyte by ion exchange is craft-complex, high-cost and time-consuming.

Inspired by molten salt post-treatment of thermally expanded graphene powders [33], herein, we developed molten salt reduction of GO films and powders separated by carbon nanodots (CNDs). As expected, the incorporation of CNDs realized the goal of opening channels between GO layers, preventing restack of graphene layers both in powders and films. Surprisingly, it was found that CNDs disappeared during the pyrolysis in the molten salt. Consequently, the CND-sacrificed, N-doped graphene films (denoted as (CND)nGF) still exhibited higher specific capacitances in an alkaline aqueous electrolyte than N-doped graphene films (nGF) without the CND assistance. Furthermore, the method of molten salt reduction combining with incorporation of CNDs exhibited advantages to surpass the progresses aforementioned. Firstly, highly dispersed small-sized CND aqueous colloids ensured the homogeneous assembly with GO, which shows the superiority to other hard substances like carbon black. Secondly, CNDs assisted molten salt of NaNH2 penetrating into the graphene gallery and subsequently opening the channel between graphene layers, especially for bulk graphene films, which avoided the complex ion exchange processes. Thirdly, the disappearance of non-capacitive CNDs during pyrolysis in the molten salt averted the decrease in the specific capacitance of the entire films, which unprecedentedly overcome the drawback of hard separators. As a result, (CND)nGF also exhibited higher capacitive performance in an alkaline aqueous electrolyte than hydrazine vapor-treated graphene films, and outperformed graphene films produced by direct pyrolysis and those with other reducing agents, which breaks the dark that the unqualified capacitive performance always accompany reduction of GO films. A specific cell capacitance of 50.5 F g−1 was achieved at 0.25 A g−1 in a two-electrode supercapacitor, corresponding to 202 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1 for a single electrode. The free-standing films in the flexible devices showed better capability to resist bending than activated carbon [34]. The supercapattery based on the corresponding graphene powder (designated as (CND)nGP) exhibited a specific capacity of 139 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 when the mass loading was set to the near-commercial value of 11.1 mg cm−2 [35,36].

The metal-organic framework of ZIF-8 was synthesized by an ultrasonication assisted precipitation method [37,38]. 7.435 g of zinc nitrate hexahydrate was dissolved in 500 mL of methanol until the solution was homogeneous. Simultaneously, 2.052 g of 2-methylimidazole and 3.64 g of triethylamine were dissolved in another 500 mL of methanol until the solution was uniform. The methanol solution of zinc nitrate hexahydrate was rapidly added to the other solution, which was subsequently subjected to ultrasonication at 600 W for 3 min. After aging for 4 h, the mixture was centrifuged, and the resulting solid was washed with methanol. The obtained milky-white ZIF-8 was obtained after drying at 80 °C for 4 h.

ZIF-8 was further heated at a rate of 8 K min−1 and held at 500 °C for 10 h to form a black-red solid during the calcination process under a nitrogen flow rate of 80 mL min−1. The resulting product was stirred in 5 mL of concentrated hydrochloric acid and 30 mL of distilled water for 2 h. Then, the liquid was purified by dialysis through a membrane (molecular weight cutoff 8–14 kDa) and diluted to form a CND colloid with a concentration of 0.8 mg mL−1.

GO was prepared from natural graphite according to the modified Hummers’ method. GO aqueous colloids (1 mg mL−1) were obtained by ultrasonication for 2 h. After the pH was adjusted to 7, 50 mL of the GO colloid and 12.5 mL of the CND colloid were mixed. After mixing, the pH of the mixture, which is slightly deviated from 7, was again adjusted to 7. The resulting CND/GO dispersion (62.5 mL) was then vacuum filtered through a cellulose acetate filter membrane (0.22 μm). The CND/GO film labeled as CND/GOF was obtained by removing the cellulose acetate filter membrane soaked in acetone for 8 h. To facilitate the subsequent processing and assembly of supercapacitors, the entire film was cut into circular films with a diameter of 10 mm. A pure GO film labeled as GOF without the addition of CND was obtained by filtering 60 mL of GO dispersion. The circular films were covered with NaNH2 in a crucible and then heated at 350 °C for 48 h in an Ar flow at a rate of 80 mL min−1 in a tube furnace. The molten NaNH2 treated film was obtained by careful washing with water and hydrochloric acid to remove residual NaNH2 and by-products in a fume hood and then drying at 60 °C for 12 h. The molten NaNH2 treated films with CNDs were dubbed as (CND)nGF, while the films without CND were as nGF. The thick films were also prepared with 4 vol of the CND/GO or pure GO dispersions as precursors, which were designated as (CND)nGTF and nGTF, respectively.

The CND/GO powder was obtained by freeze-drying the dispersion of CNDs/GO with the same mass ratio of 5. A mixture of NaNH2 and CND/GO powder with a ratio of 4.5:1 was placed in a crucible and then heated at 350 °C for 48 h in an Ar flow at a rate of 80 mL min−1 in a tube furnace. Finally, the molten NaNH2 treated powder was obtained by washing with water and drying in an oven at 60 °C. The powder samples with CNDs were labeled as (CND)nGP, while those without CNDs were as nGP.

The microstructures and morphologies were characterized by SEM (Apreo S HiVac, Thermo Fisher) and TEM (JEM-F200, JEOL, 200 kV). Organic elemental analyses (OEA) were performed by a Germany-Elementar-UNICUBE in CHN and O modes. Raman spectra were recorded by a Horiba HR80 spectrometer using an excitation wavelength of 514 nm. The chemical composition of the samples was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250XI). The N2 adsorption/desorption measurements were carried out at 77 K using automatic volumetric adsorption equipment (ASAP2460).

The working electrodes were prepared by roll-pressing the circular films onto stainless steel current collectors. The areal mass loading of the thin film was 1.3 mg cm−2, while that of the thick film was 6.8 mg cm−2. The electrochemical performance was measured with a 6 mol L−1 KOH electrolyte in a three-electrode cell with a platinum foil and a Hg/HgO electrode serving as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. A symmetrical two-electrode supercapacitor was fabricated by assembling two films with equal masses and a glass fiber separator.

The poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA)-KOH gel was used as the electrolyte in quasi-solid-state flexible supercapacitors. 3 g PVA powder was added to 20 mL of water and heated to 90 °C under vigorous stirring until the solution became transparent. Subsequently, 3 g KOH in 10 mL of water was added to the PVA solution, which was subsequently stirred until a clear gel state was obtained. Two nickel foam electrodes loaded with (CND)nGF and a separator were immersed in the gel electrolyte for 30 min and then cured at 50 °C in an oven for another 30 min. Finally, the two film electrodes of (CND)nGF and the separator were assembled and heat-sealed to create a flexible film supercapacitor.

The (CND)nGFs were directly used as electrodes. The powder electrodes were prepared by mixing 80 % of (CND)nGP, 10 % of acetylene black and 10 % of polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) or polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) by weight. The areal mass loading of the active material in the electrode with PTFE binder was 11.1 mg cm−2. The CR2032 coin supercapattery was made in an Ar-filled glove box (VAC OMN) with (CND)nGP as the positive electrode and Li metal as the negative electrode separated by a glass fiber membrane with 1 mol L−1 LiPF6 in ethylene carbonate (EC) and diethyl carbonate (DEC) (1:1 v/v) as the electrolyte.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed using an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660 E). Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) at various specific currents and constant current intermittent titration technique (GITT) at 0.1 A g−1 with an intermittent charging and discharging time of 1 min and a shelf time of 10 min were carried out using a battery analyzer (Neware CT-4008 T). The specific capacitance, capacity, energy and power were calculated according to Equations S1–S6.

Fig. S1 depicts the TEM image of the product derived from ZIF-8. Many of the products exhibited sizes less than 10 nm, indicating the production of CNDs by pyrolysis of ZIF-8 at 500 °C. Although the derived Zn species during the pyrolysis as templates separated the carbon components, this medium pyrolysis temperature was a more key factor for preventing the agglomeration of the derived carbon [37]. It can be verified by the fact that bulk carbon could be yielded at an elevated temperature of 800 °C [38]. The OEA indicated that CNDs comprised C, O and N elements, the ratio of which was 3.65:1.00:1.93. The N element in CNDs were from ZIF-8. Table S1 shows that the residual Zn atomic content in the CNDs was 3.52 %. The inset of Fig. S1 revealed an interplanar spacing of 0.210 nm, corresponding to that of the graphite (100) plane. In Fig. S2, a very thin GO sheet is presented. Upon mixing CNDs with GO, the resulting colloids were stable at least for 15 days under different pH values, as shown in Fig. S3. It was evident that the perfectly circular CNDs were monodispersed on the surface of GO, as shown in Fig. 1 a and b. The inset displays an interplanar spacing of 0.210 nm, consistent with that in Fig. S1. Fig. 1c shows that the sizes of the CNDs range from 4 to 12 nm, with an average size of 7.8 ± 1.5 nm. The monodispersed morphology and uniform size distribution of CNDs on GO suggest that GO effectively prevented the aggregation of CNDs.

After vacuum filtration of the colloids comprising GO with or without CNDs, the corresponding films were obtained. When increasing the CND:GO ratio from 0.2 to 0.3, the composite film cracked. CND/GOF exhibited an impressively coarser surface than GOF from the top view due to the incorporation of CNDs (Fig. S4a and b). Both pure GOF and CND/GOF possessed compact structures due to the face-to-face orientation of the 2D GO sheets from the side view (Fig. S4d and e). After annealing with molten NaNH2, the surfaces of nGF and (CND)nGF were distorted due to expansion in the molten salt (Fig. S4c and Fig. 1d). Compared to that of nGF, the surface of (CND)nGF was relatively flat in local morphology. However, the addition of CNDs into (CND)nGF resulted in more pores on the surface due to the increased amount of escaped gases. The thicknesses of nGF and (CND)nGF obviously increased (Fig. S4f and Fig. 1e), compared to those of GOF and CND/GOF. These pores in the films from the side view arose from escape of the gases generated during the reduction of GO and CND/GO with molten NaNH2 at the elevated temperature, which could open the gallery between graphene layers for ion transports. However, it perhaps caused the relatively low electronic conductivity of graphene film in the normal direction. The problem may be alleviated by crosslinked graphene layers [18], which is worth being further studied in our subsequent work. No CNDs were seen on the surface of the defective graphene in (CND)nGF, as shown in Fig. 1f and S5a, which represented the high probability. However, compared to the morphology in Fig. 1a, ignorable CNDs with extremely low density could be seen on the graphene in Fig. S5b after repeated TEM observation of the (CND)nGF specimen. Moreover, no products remained when the bald CNDs were subjected to molten NaNH2 at the same temperature and time. The elimination of CNDs in the molten salt treatment was attributed to the small-sized carbon etched by the high oxygen content in CNDs and released CO from GO treated with molten NaNH2.

The powders of GOP and CND/GOP were also subjected to treatment with molten NaNH2. Fig. S6 illustrates the TEM image of nGP, where the defects were unevenly distributed. Fig. 1g depicts the TEM image of (CND)nGP after molten NaNH2 treatment, revealing numerous defects on the graphene without any CNDs. Clearly, the defect density of (CND)nGP was greater than that of nGP. The fringe distance of the indicated defects was measured at 0.350 nm. Intercalated CNDs could facilitate the access of molten NaNH2 into the channels between individual GO sheets. In turn, molten NaNH2 etched CNDs to leave more defects in (CND)nGP. The increase in defects was expected to result in higher electrochemical activity in (CND)nGP, indicating that CNDs may play an indispensable role in high-performance graphene films and powders.

Fig. S7 shows the nitrogen adsorption isotherm of (CND)nGP, which corresponded to a type-IV curve with a hysteresis loop. The surface area of (CND)nGP was calculated to be 63 m2 g−1 by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller method. The total pore volume was 0.096 cm3 g−1, which gave a theoretical apparent density of 1.82 g cm−3, indicative of a high packing density of the (CND)nGP electrode. The pore size distribution by Barrett-Joyner-Halenda method in the inset of Fig. S7 showed that the pore of (CND)nGP concentrated at 3.8 nm within the mesopore ranges.

The Raman spectrum of CND (Fig. S8) reveals that the D and G bands were located around 1315 and 1576 cm−1, respectively. Notably, the peak at 1437 cm−1 was the characteristic peak of CND [39,40]. The shoulder at 1175 cm−1 originated from sp3 hybridized orbitals, resembling that of nanocrystalline diamond with small grain sizes, sp3 rich phases or hexagonal diamonds [41]. Fig. S9a and 2a present the Raman spectra of GOF, CND/GOF, nGF and (CND)nGF. The D bands in all the samples at approximately 1350 cm−1 are attributed to defects or disordered scattering, while the G bands around 1590 cm−1 are associated with vibration signals from sp2-bonded carbon. The D to G peak intensity ratio (I D/I G) for CND/GOF was 0.92, almost the same as 0.93 for GOF, indicating that a small number of CNDs barely influence the Raman signal of the film. After treatment with molten NaNH2, the I D/I G ratios for (CND)nGF and nGF increased to 1.19 and 1.08 respectively, suggesting that the reduction process increases the number of defects. The slightly greater I D/I G ratio of (CND)nGF, compared to that of nGF, implies that more defects were left in (CND)nGF after sacrificing CNDs by more thorough reduction owing to the opened channels in the film by intercalated CNDs for access of the molten NaNH2.

Fig. S9b and 2b present the XPS survey spectra for GOF, CND/GOF, nGF and (CND)nGF, all of which depict the C 1s, O 1s and N 1s signals. The semiquantitative C:O:N atomic ratios of the GOF and CND/GOF surfaces were 2.12:1.00:0.11 and 2.00:1.00:0.14, respectively. After treatment with molten NaNH2, the surface C:O:N atomic ratios of nGF and (CND)nGF changed to 12.2:1.00:1.15 and 11.2:1.00:0.96, respectively. The significant increase in the C:O and N:O atomic ratios demonstrates that most of the oxygen functional groups were reduced and N doping was realized in the films with molten NaNH2. In the C 1s spectrum of GOF (Fig. S9c), four distinct peaks could be seen at 284.6, 286.7, 287.8 and 288.8 eV, corresponding to the C–C, C–O, C=O and –COO bonds, respectively. Notably, compared to GOF, CND/GOF exhibited a more intense C–O peak (Fig. 2 c), and a new small C–N peak emerged at 287.0 eV. These results indicate that both the N and O contents increased upon the incorporation of CNDs derived from N-containing ZIF-8. Due to condensation and decomposition reactions, the C=O and –COO peaks decreased, and most of the C–O groups were removed after annealing with molten NaNH2, indicating a significant reduction in the oxygen content for nGF and (CND)nGF. The N 1s peak was negligible in Fig. S9d for GOF. Three peaks in the N 1s spectrum of CND/GOF could be seen at approximately 398.2, 400.0, and 401.4 eV, corresponding to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and quaternary N, respectively [42]. After annealing with molten NaNH2, pyrrolic nitrogen became the predominant species in (CND)nGF, which was more stable than other nitrogen-containing compounds. The strong nucleophile NaNH2 could attack the epoxy groups bonded to carbon atoms in GO, leading to the formation of the amino-alcohol-Na. Subsequently, the species further interacted with neighboring hydroxyl or epoxy groups to form pyridinic N and quaternary N [33]. Theoretical calculations demonstrated that pyrrolic-N could produce electronegativities and defects in the carbon structure, increasing the active sites and providing an electron deficiency [43]. In addition to the three peaks, both nGF and (CND)nGF exhibited another amino-N peak at 399.4 eV (Figs. S9d and 2d), arising from the reaction between GO and NaNH2.

In a 6 M KOH electrolyte, CV and GCD measurements were conducted on nGF and (CND)nGF using three-electrode cells within the potential range of −1 to 0 V vs. Hg/HgO electrode. Fig. 3 a illustrates the cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of nGF and (CND)nGF at 10 mV s−1. The CV shapes displayed relatively intact rectangles, wherein higher absolute values of specific currents could be seen at high potentials in the negative current scan compared to other graphene or carbon-based supercapacitors [10,44,45], suggesting that both nGF and (CND)nGF exhibited mainly ideal double-layer capacitance characteristics. Compared to CV of nGF, the CV of (CND)nGF showed very broad oxidation and reduction peaks at −0.703 and −0.814 V respectively, associated with the residual hydrophilic functional groups in (CND)nGF. The difference between them was only 0.111 V, indicating that the redox processes were nearly reversible. Fig. 3b shows the GCD curves of nGF and (CND)nGF at 1 A g−1. Considering that the coulombic efficiency should not exceed 100 %, the specific capacitance was calculated according to the shorter branch in the GCD curves [45]. (CND)nGF achieved a specific capacitance of 246 F g−1, higher than 218 F g−1 for nGF. The coulombic efficiency of (CND)nGF was 94.6 %, while that of nGF was 93.4 %. On one hand, the sacrificed CNDs eliminated the negative weight impact of the non-active component in the electrode materials. On the other hand, more defects left after sacrificing CNDs which facilitated greater access of NaNH2 into the GO layers. These two aspects were dominantly responsible for higher capacitance and more reversible performance of (CND)nGF. Fig. S10 presents CVs of (CND)nGF at different scan rates. When the scan rate increased to 100 mV s−1, the rectangular shape was retained, suggesting that most of the charge storage might occur not only on the limited surfaces but also in the space between graphene layers even at a high scan rate, which was better than common situations [46]. Fig. 3c shows the GCD curves of (CND)nGF at different specific currents. When the specific current increased to 10 A g−1 (10 times higher), (CND)nGF still delivered a high specific capacitance of 216 F g−1, retaining 87.8 % of the specific capacitance at 1 A g−1.

In addition to incorporating CNDs in the graphene films, molten NaNH2 reduction was also demonstrated to be highly beneficial for improving the capacitive performance. As shown in Fig. 3d and S11, at the same specific current of 1 A g−1, molten NaNH2 treated (CND)nGF delivered a specific capacitance of 246 F g−1, while the film obtained only by pyrolysis of CND/GOF at the same heat treatment but without molten NaNH2 had no capacitance. Replacing NaNH2 with ZnCl2 produced a film with a specific capacitance of only 4 F g−1. The much superior performance of (CND)nGF was dominantly attributed to the gas evolution during NaNH2 treatment prevented the agglomeration of graphene. Additionally, the resulting defects and N doping, especially from pyridinic N, also facilitated the ion diffusion and produced pseudocapacitance. When subjected to corrosive hydroiodic acid treatment at 100 °C for 1 h, CND/GOF was reduced to a graphene-based film, which exhibited a small specific capacitance of 80 F g−1. Even if the CND/GOF was reduced with toxic hydrazine hydrate vapor in a sealed autoclave at 90 °C for 10 h, the resulting graphene-based film possessed a specific capacitance of 192 F g−1, evidently lower than that of (CND)nGF. The comparison of the films obtained by different methods indicates the advantages of the molten NaNH2 treatment for high-performance graphene-based films.

Symmetrical two-electrode supercapacitors were assembled with 6 mol L−1 KOH electrolytes. CVs of the supercapacitor based on (CND)nGF show nearly perfect rectangles at all scan rates (Fig. 4 a) due to the tight structure of the two-electrode cells. Fig. 4b also illustrates that the highly symmetrical triangular GCD curves were well maintained at different specific currents for the (CND)nGF-based supercapacitor. These results indicate that the electrochemical behavior of the (CND)nGF-based aqueous supercapacitor was dominated by the electric double layer capacitance mechanism. The specific cell capacitance of the (CND)nGF-based supercapacitor was 48.9 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1, corresponding to 196 F g−1 for a single electrode at 1 A g−1. At 0.25 A g−1, the specific cell capacitance reached 50.6 F g−1, equivalent to 202 F g−1 for a single electrode at 0.5 A g−1, which is slightly smaller than that of (CND)nGF in a three-electrode cell. Fig. S12 presents the cycling stability of the (CND)nGF-based supercapacitor at 2 A g−1. After 10,000 and 20,000 cycles, the capacitance retentions were 77.1 % and 68.8 %, respectively. The coulombic efficiency increased from the initial 95.0 %–100 % with the increasing cycle number.

Thick graphene-based films with higher mass loadings are strongly required in practical applications. After expanding the dispersion by a factor of four, nGTF and (CND)nGTF were obtained. Consequently, the areal density of (CND)nGTF increased from 1.3 to 6.8 mg cm−2. Fig. S13 shows CVs of the supercapacitors based on (CND)nGTF and nGTF. The (CND)nGTF cell possessed larger currents at the same scan rate of 10 mV s−1. At 0.5 A g−1, the specific cell capacitance of the (CND)nGTF-based supercapacitor was 42.6 F g−1, while that of the nGTF-based one was only 36.0 F g−1, according to Fig. 4c. Fig. 4d compares the specific cell capacitances of the supercapacitor based on (CND)nGTF with those of nGTF at various specific currents. The specific cell capacitance of (CND)nGTF could reach 48.2 F g−1 at 0.25 A g−1, corresponding to 193 F g−1 for a single electrode at 0.5 A g−1, which was 95.5 % of that of (CND)nGF. Therefore, an increase in the membrane thickness seldom resulted in capacitive attenuation, indicating that the molten NaNH2-treated graphene-based films are promising materials for practical applications. When the specific current increased from 0.25 to 2 A g−1, the specific cell capacitance of (CND)nGTF changed from 1.13 to 2.4 times that of nGTF. The significantly superior performance of the (CND)nGTF-based supercapacitor at all specific currents exhibited the advantage of incorporating CNDs, which could facilitate the access of molten NaNH2 into the spaces between individual graphene layers.

Two-electrode flexible quasi-solid-state supercapacitors were assembled using the (CND)nGF electrodes with PVA-KOH gel electrolytes. The CVs of the flexible supercapacitor exhibited almost perfect rectangular shapes (Fig. 5 a). According to Fig. 5b, the specific cell capacitance of the device was calculated to be 48.7 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1, equivalent to 195 F g−1 for a single electrode at 1 A g−1, which is the same as 196 F g−1 for the supercapacitor in the KOH aqueous electrolyte. When bent at angles of 0°, 45°, 90°, 135° and 180°, the flexible device exhibited little change in both the CVs and GCD curves, as shown in Fig. 5c and d. At 0.5 A g−1, the device bent at 180° had a specific cell capacitance of 48.4 F g−1, maintaining 99.4 % of the initial specific capacitance at 0°. To verify the mechanical flexibility of the device, the harsh 180° bending experiments were repeatedly performed. Impressively, the device also maintained 70.4 % of its initial capacitance after 180° bending for 1000 cycles (Fig. 5e), showcasing much better flexibility than the activated carbon-based device (with the same retention for only 400 cycles) [34]. The changes in the EIS spectra revealed the reason for the capacitive deterioration with repeated bending at 180°. The equivalent series resistance (R s) of the unfolded device was as low as 1.77 Ω. After 20 bending cycles, the EIS spectrum overlaid that before bending. R s turned to be 4.60 Ω after bending 1000 times, suggesting that the film was not destroyed. However, the charge transfer resistance (R ct) increased obviously after 200 cycles of bending, meaning that ion diffusion clogged within the distorted channels between graphene layers, which is one of the main reasons for the capacitive deterioration. These results illuminate the excellent capacitance and relatively good flexibility of the quasi-solid-state supercapacitor using (CND)nGF electrodes.

Generally, supercapacitors possess smaller specific energies than lithium-ion batteries. However, the short cycling lifetimes and poor rate performance are the main challenges of lithium-ion batteries due to the limitations of traditional positive electrode materials, such as LiCoO2 and LiFePO4. It is proposed that these positive electrode materials could be replaced with capacitive materials [3,47]. Typically, Li | 1 mol L−1 LiPF6 | (CND)nGF supercapatteries were assembled to meet the high specific energy requirement. Fig. 6 a shows a distorted CV rectangle of the (CND)nGF-based supercapattery at 5 mV s−1 after activation. However, the currents at the low potentials in the positive scan were still low, suggesting that the PF6 − ions were hardly electrochemically absorbed on the surface of (CND)nGF at the relatively low potentials. According to Fig. 6b, (CND)nGF delivered a maximum specific capacitance of 73 F g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 (0.7 C for LiCoO2) in the LiPF6 electrolytes, corresponding to 61 mAh g−1 which is much lower than 140 mAh g−1 for LiCoO2 [48].

The corresponding (CND)nGPs were used to replace (CND)nGFs as the positive electrodes. When PVDF was used as the binder, the mass loading of the thin (CND)nGP positive electrode was only 0.47 mg cm−2. As shown in Fig. 6b, the (CND)nGP-based supercapattery with the PVDF binder exhibited a specific capacity of 184 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1, which is only 10.7 % smaller than that of molten salt treated thermally exfoliated graphene [33]. Toward practical applications, when PTFE was used as the binder, the mass loading of the (CND)nGP positive electrode increased to 11.1 mg cm−2 which was very closed to the requirements of commercial supercapacitors and lithium-ion batteries. Fig. 6a demonstrates that (CND)nGP had a stable potential window of 1.5–4.5 V. Compared to the nGP-based supercapatteries, the (CND)nGP one presented a larger specific current in Fig. 6a, corresponding to a higher capacitance. This finding verifies that the sacrificed CNDs played a vital role in promoting the capacitive performance. The smaller currents at low potentials were not improved when (CND)nGF was replaced with (CND)nGP. The broad current peaks in these CVs are possibly due to electrochemical reactions contributed by the residual oxygen and nitrogen functional groups in graphene. At 0.1 A g−1, the (CND)nGP-based supercapattery with the PTFE binder delivered a specific capacitance of 134 mAh g−1, while the nGP exhibited 70 mAh g−1 (Fig. 6b). The superior performance obtained by (CND)nGP was attributed to the incorporation of CNDs, which prevented the stacking of the individual graphene layers, facilitated the penetration of NaNH2 between layers, and hence resulted in uniform and more defects as active sites [49]. Although the specific capacity is a key property for electrode materials, the areal capacity is an equally important parameter for commercial applications. Despite not having the highest specific capacity, the (CND)nGP electrode with the PTFE binder achieved the highest areal capacity of 1.49 mAh cm−2 at 1.11 mA cm−2 (Fig. 6c). This capacity far exceeds the 0.31 mAh cm−2 at 0.3C (0.042 A g−1) for bare LiCoO2 [50] and the 0.95 mAh cm−2 at 1C (0.140 A g−1) for electrospun LiCoO2 [51]. Fig. 6d illustrates the GCD curves of the (CND)nGP cell with the PTFE binder at 0.1 A g−1 for the first 10 cycles. The (CND)nGP cell achieved an initial discharge specific capacity of 127 mAh g−1 with an initial coulombic efficiency of 91 %, which could supply 16 light-emitting diodes (Fig. S14). By the 10th cycle, the discharge specific capacity reached at 134 mAh g−1 and the coulombic efficiency increased to 96 %. The nearly 100 % coulombic efficiency in the first cycle is one of the advantages of supercapatteries compared to lithium-ion batteries, indicating excellent reversibility of lithium ions in (CND)nGP during lithiation and delithiation processes. Fig. 6e displays the rate capability of the (CND)nGP and nGP cells at various current densities from 0.1 to 5 A g−1. The (CND)nGP exhibited a higher specific capacity of 139 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 compared to nGP (70 mAh g−1). Even at a high specific current of 5 A g−1, (CND)nGP still provided a specific capacity of 18 mAh g−1, while only 10 mAh g−1 could be achieved at such a high specific current for nGP. The superior performance of (CND)nGP relative to nGP was mainly attributed to more uniform defects left in (CND)nGP by sacrificed CNDs. The increased rate capability also suggested that (CND)nGP possessed superior ion transport performance, compared to nGP. Fig. S15 presents the cycling performance of (CND)nGP at 0.5 A g−1. After 1000 repeated charge/discharge cycles, the capacity retention was maintained at 68.8 % of the initial value, with coulombic efficiencies consistently close to 100 % throughout the cyclic measurements. XPS and SEM were performed to analyze the surface chemical properties and morphology of the (CND)nGP positive electrode after 1000 cycles which was cleaned with dimethyl carbonate to remove the electrolyte. The survey XPS spectrum of (CND)nGP in Fig. S16a still exhibited the prominent C 1s, O 1s, Li 1s, F 1s and P 2p peaks. The F 1s XPS spectrum in Fig. S16b was deconvoluted into the C–F and C–F2 peaks, which were partially arising from the PTFE binder. A small but clear P 2p peaks in Fig. S16c could be assigned to metaphosphate which was formed by the reactions among electrode, electrolyte and solvents. It inferred that the partial F signals were also from the reactions with electrolyte. These side reactions deteriorated the electrochemical performance after long-term cycling. The Li 1s signal after cycling is shown in Fig. S16d, indicating the residual inserted lithium ions in (CND)nGP. Fig. S17 shows the morphological changes of the positive electrode before and after the long-term cycling. Comparison of the morphology of the fresh sample (Fig. S17 a and b) with that of the positive electrode after 1000 cycles (Fig. S17 c and d) revealed that evident cracks emerged on the surface of the flat electrode material after cycling due to the continuous lithium insertion and desertion during cycling. The morphological change impeded the diffusion path of the lithium ions and decreased the electric conductivity for the electrode, ultimately leading to attenuation in battery capacity. The other two supercapatteries with mass loadings of 12.7 and 7.9 mg cm−2 could retain 64.1 % and 58.7 % after 2500 and 2000 cycles at 0.5 and 2 A g−1, respectively, as shown in Fig. S18.

The performance of the (CND)nGF-based supercapatteries was not ideal, possibly due to the sluggish kinetics of organic ions in the organic solution in the tight channels between graphene layers, especially at low potentials. Fortunately, a maximum specific capacity of 184 mAh g−1 with high initial coulombic efficiency was obtained by (CND)nGP, suggesting that materials treated with molten salt have the potential to replace LiCoO2 in the positive electrodes of lithium-ion batteries. The superior properties of (CND)nGP relative to nGP, whether in capacity or rate capability, were mainly attributed to the more defects produced by thorough penetration and reaction of NaNH2 with CND-incorporated graphene layers. Even with a mass loading increased to the near-commercial required 11.1 mg cm−2, (CND)nGP still delivered a specific capacity of 139 mAh g−1. The decreased specific capacity with increased mass loading is a universal phenomenon [52]. However, the areal capacity reached 1.54 mAh cm−2, which is much higher than that of LiCoO2. Fig. S19 shows the specific powers as a function of the specific energies of the (CND)nGF-based aqueous and flexible supercapacitors and (CND)nGP-based supercapattery. The (CND)nGF-based aqueous supercapacitor delivered a maximum specific energy of 7.6 Wh kg−1. It is worth noting that the specific energies of the (CND)nGF-based supercapacitor were also based on the entire electrode because no additives were added in the films. If considering the positive effect of the film electrode instead of a conventional electrode comprising 80 % active component and 20 % additives, the maximum equivalent specific energy of the (CND)nGF was supposed to be 9.4 Wh kg−1, which is obviously higher than 6.8 Wh kg−1 for pomelo peel [10]. A maximum specific energy of 369 Wh kg−1 was achieved by the (CND)nGP-based supercapattery at 273 W kg−1. The maximum for (CND)nGP with a near commercial loading of 11.1 mg cm−2 is much higher than 246 Wh kg−1 for NGF-0 (2 mg cm−2) [53], higher than 334 Wh kg−1 for rGO/AC (0.9–2.8 mg cm−2) [54] and nearly the same as 374 Wh kg−1 for a-HC-4 (3 mg cm−2) [55]. These results indicate that molten salt-treated graphene films and powders merit further study.

To further investigate the energy storage mechanism of (CND)nGP and nGP, the diffusion coefficients of Li+ in these materials were investigated using the constant current intermittent titration technique (GITT) (Fig. S20). Fig. 6f shows the estimated Li+ diffusion coefficients during the charging and discharging processes. In order to rule out the influence of the self-discharging, it was reasonable to analyze the Li+ diffusion coefficients during the charging course. The Li+ diffusion coefficients in (CND)nGP ranged from 1.1 × 10−7 to 2.18 × 10−9 cm2 s−1, while those in nGP from 4.63 × 10−8 to 5.47 × 10−10 cm2 s−1 at various potential or time. Typically, the Li+ diffusion coefficients in (CND)nGP and nGP at 3 V were 2.91 × 10−8 and 1.33 × 10−8 cm2 s−1, respectively. In fact, the Li+ diffusion coefficients in (CND) nGP in all potentials were significantly higher than those of nGP, indicating that the sacrificed CNDs still efficiently prevented the stack of graphene and hence helped Li ion diffusion kinetics in (CND) nGP.

We developed molten salt method to prepare graphene-based films from CND-incorporated GO films. This method, compared to producing films from reduced graphene colloids, overcome the low utilization of raw materials and avoid the complexity of ion exchange processes. The incorporation of CNDs opened channels between GO layers in the films. During pyrolysis in the molten salt, CNDs disappeared, which increased the number of defects and prevented the decrease in the performance of the entire films. (CND)nGF exhibited a high specific capacitance of 246 F g−1 in a three-electrode cell with an alkaline aqueous electrolyte, surpassing those of nGF without CND assistance and hydrazine vapor treated graphene films, and orders of magnitude greater than those of the graphene films obtained by direct pyrolysis of CND-incorporated GO and with activating agents or other reducing agents. The superior performance of (CND)nGF is attributed to the greater number of defects left when sacrificing CNDs because of the increased access of molten salt in the channels between graphene layers. A specific cell capacitance of 50.5 F g−1 was obtained at 0.25 A g−1 in a two-electrode cell, corresponding to 202 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1 for a single electrode. The flexible device based on (CND)nGF possessed a specific cell capacitance of 48.7 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1, equivalent to 195 F g−1 for a single electrode at 1 A g−1. The flexible device demonstrated better resistance to bending than that based on activated carbon. The (CND)nGP-based supercapattery exhibited 139 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 when the mass loading was 11.1 mg cm−2, very close to commercial requirements.
